figures.caption: Fig. 1. Autonomous robot navigation under the proposed theoretical architecture. Operator-defined safety rules are translated into Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications ϕ1, ϕ2, ϕ3 with time-invariant preferences embedded into the environment cost map and time-varying rules integrated into the planning component and monitored at runtime.

Fig. 3. State-dependent navigation illustrates normal versus low-battery mode. The finite-state controller switches specifications ϕ1 − ϕ3, which changes which candidate paths are treated as preferred (green), valid and less-preferred/tolerated (yellow), or violating (red).
TABLE II TABLE DESCRIBING RULES AND SOFT OPERATOR-BASED PREFERENCES FOR BOTH ROBOT STATES.

TABLE I COMPARISON BETWEEN OUR ARCHITECTURE AND REPRESENTATIVE NAVIGATION APPROACHES. COLUMNS INDICATE THE ROLE OF VLMS, SENSING MODALITY, WHETHER STL OR OTHER FORMAL VERIFICATION IS USED AT PLANNING OR RUN TIME, AND PLANNER TYPE.

Fig. 2. Overall theoretical architecture of our VLM-grounded safe navigation stack. We obtain operator-provided feature costs and operator-provided safety rules from mission specifications and hand-translate the rules into STL specifications that are manually decomposed into relevant features for the VLM to parse in a RGB camera image. Combined with the feature costs, we construct an environment cost map for all temporally-invariant specifications. The time-varying specifications are incorporated into a runtime assurance layer that iteratively validates paths proposed by the path planner.
